metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a proving command to AC2 — e.g. extend the existing SHA-pin lint/grep step to also assert `persist-credentials: false` is present on every `actions/checkout` step in `release.yml`.
findings.finding: AC2 ('Every release checkout sets persist-credentials: false') has no described proving command — no grep, lint assertion, or CI check is specified to verify this property, unlike AC1 which names a lint/grep check.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2: 'Every release checkout sets persist-credentials: false'
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC2: 'verified by the same workflow-lint step used for AC1, which also greps for `persist-credentials: false` on every `actions/checkout` block in `release.yml` and fails if absent'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Revise the build-tool version-match AC to specify the concrete verification mechanism — e.g. use `pip-compile --generate-hashes` output parsed to extract only `package==version` lines (stripping hashes/comments) and diff that against `pip list --format=freeze`, or replace the diff approach with `pip install --dry-run --require-hashes -r <lock>` exit-code check — so the assertion is implementable as written.
findings.finding: The AC for build-tool version verification specifies `pip list --format=freeze > tool-versions.txt` and 'a CI lint step asserting the installed versions match the pinned lock exactly (a diff against the lock's pinned versions must be empty)', but `pip list --format=freeze` emits `Package==version` lines with no hashes, while `pip-compile --generate-hashes` lock files contain hash annotations and inline comments — the formats are structurally incompatible for a direct diff, so the 'diff must be empty' assertion as written will never pass without a normalization step that is not specified.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria: 'Release build tools install from a reviewed hash-locked file… a CI lint step asserting the installed versions match the pinned lock exactly (a diff against the lock's pinned versions must be empty)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'a diff against the lock's pinned versions must be empty' with: 'a CI check that runs `pip install --dry-run --require-hashes --no-deps -r <lock>` against the already-installed environment exits zero, confirming all installed versions satisfy the locked hashes.' This is directly implementable and does not require format normalization.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how the AC5 mutation-test CI check is implemented — e.g. a dedicated CI job that downloads the pinned archive, corrupts one byte, runs the verify step, and asserts exit code 1 — so the executor has a concrete deliverable.
findings.finding: AC5's verification method ('a CI check that substituting a different archive for the pinned one causes the step to exit non-zero') is described as an outcome but not as a concrete step, script, or test job — leaving an executor without a clear implementation target for the mutation test.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC5: 'verified by a CI check that substituting a different archive...'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete capture-and-assert step for each evidence item in AC6: specify which workflow step writes action SHAs, MCP publisher version+digest, and wheel/sdist digests to a named artifact file, and add a CI lint assertion (e.g. grep/jq) that all four categories are non-empty in that file.
findings.finding: AC6 ('Release evidence records all action SHAs, build-tool versions, the MCP publisher version + digest, and the wheel/sdist digests') names four distinct evidence items but provides no concrete proving command to verify all four are present and captured — tool-versions.txt (from AC3) covers build-tool versions, but action SHAs, MCP publisher digest, and wheel/sdist digests have no specified capture step or artifact assertion.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC6: 'Release evidence records all action SHAs, build-tool versions, the MCP publisher version + digest, and the wheel/sdist digests'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the 'repo already SHA-pins actions' premise: `gerrit-verify.yaml` and `_optionality.yml` SHA-pin only **third-party** actions; first-party `actions/checkout@v7` and `actions/setup-python@v6` are tag-pinned in those files too — clarify that this story establishes SHA-pinning for ALL actions (including first-party) in `release.yml`, and that the lint check must cover first-party action refs.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'The repo already SHA-pins actions in `gerrit-verify.yaml` and `_optionality.yml`' as the established convention that `release.yml` is 'inconsistent' with — but probing shows both files use tag-based references for first-party GitHub actions (`actions/setup-python@v6` at gerrit-verify.yaml:75; `actions/checkout@v7` and `actions/setup-python@v6` throughout `_optionality.yml`). The SHA-pin convention in those files applies only to third-party actions (`lfreleng-actions/*`, `im-open/*`). This mischaracterizes the existing convention, which could mislead the implementer about (a) the scope of the lint check they need to write (is `actions/checkout` expected to be SHA-pinned already, or is this story adding that?), and (b) whether lint infrastructure already exists that can be extended.
findings.location: Why section — 'The repo already SHA-pins actions in `gerrit-verify.yaml` and `_optionality.yml`, so `release.yml` is inconsistent with the repo's own convention.'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'The repo already SHA-pins actions in `gerrit-verify.yaml` and `_optionality.yml`' with 'The repo SHA-pins third-party actions in `gerrit-verify.yaml` (per its header comment) — this story extends that convention to all action references (including first-party `actions/*`) in `release.yml`.' Update the AC-1 lint-check description to explicitly state it must flag `actions/checkout`, `actions/setup-python`, `actions/upload-artifact`, and `actions/download-artifact` tag refs, not only third-party action tags.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the multi-arch SHA verification strategy explicitly: either (a) enumerate a SHA-256 per supported OS/arch pair in a structured map in the workflow (with the runner arch selecting the correct entry), or (b) require and verify an upstream `checksums.txt` (fail the step if upstream does not ship one), or (c) document the deliberate decision to lock to linux/amd64 only and remove the arch-detection logic.
findings.finding: The MCP publisher SHA-256 hard-coding mechanism is underdetermined for multi-arch runners: the existing workflow constructs the download URL from `uname -s` and `uname -m` (producing distinct binaries per OS/arch), so there is not one SHA-256 but one per OS+arch pair. The plan says to 'hard-code the SHA-256 computed at pin time' without specifying how to handle the per-arch dimension — leaving an implementer to either hard-code only linux/amd64 (breaking silently on arm64 runners) or maintain a multi-arch SHA map with no specified structure.
findings.location: What section — 'MCP publisher — download → verify → execute' / release.yml line 129 / AC line: 'the pinned SHA-256 is hard-coded in the workflow with a rationale comment'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the dynamic arch-detection with a pinned linux/amd64 URL (since ubuntu-latest runners used by release.yml are currently amd64) and document this as a deliberate scope decision with a revisit trigger when GitHub enables arm64 ubuntu-latest by default; OR add a small associative-array SHA map keyed by `${OS}_${ARCH}` so every supported arch has a verified entry and the step fails if no entry exists for the detected arch.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly state in the plan whether the MCP publisher pin is intentionally arch-fixed (x86_64/linux_amd64 only, matching `ubuntu-latest`), OR specify that per-arch URL+digest pairs must be stored and selected conditionally — resolving the single-URL/single-SHA gap introduced by replacing the current arch-dynamic download.
findings.finding: The plan specifies pinning the MCP publisher to "an exact version URL" with a single hard-coded SHA-256, but the current download in `release.yml:129` is architecture-dynamic (`uname -s` / `uname -m` with sed transforms produce arch-specific filenames like `mcp-publisher_linux_amd64.tar.gz`). A single URL and single digest can only cover one OS/arch tuple. If the runner arch or OS changes (e.g. GitHub migrates ubuntu-latest to arm64, or macOS runners are added to this job), the pinned URL and digest will not match the runner's binary — causing either a download 404 or a sha256 mismatch. The plan does not state that the pin is intentionally arch-fixed (x86_64/linux only) nor does it specify that per-arch URL+digest pairs must be stored.
findings.location: What section — MCP publisher 'download → verify → execute' steps; release.yml:129
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note in the 'What' section: 'The exact-version URL and pinned SHA-256 are arch-specific; store one `<url, sha256>` pair per supported OS/arch tuple (currently only `linux_amd64` for `ubuntu-latest`) and select the matching pair via a `case $(uname -s)/$(uname -m)` block. Update all pairs when bumping the publisher version.'

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the `--no-build-isolation` guard lives in an independent location outside `release.yml` (e.g., a `pre-commit` hook or a step in `gerrit-verify.yaml` that greps `release.yml` for the flag), so it cannot be silently removed by the same edit that removes the flag itself.
findings.finding: The lint/test step that guards `--no-build-isolation` is not located — the plan specifies that such a step must exist and fail if the flag is removed, but does not say where it lives. If the step lives inside `release.yml` itself, an implementer can silently remove both the build step and the guard in a single edit, eliminating the protection. A self-referential guard inside the file it is guarding is not a real enforcement mechanism.
findings.location: What section — 'Hatchling enforcement — decision' / AC line: 'a workflow lint/test step that fails if --no-build-isolation is removed (no free-resolution path remains)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a step to `gerrit-verify.yaml`'s `build-and-test` job (or a new `release-lint` job) that runs `grep -q 'no-build-isolation' .github/workflows/release.yml || (echo '::error::--no-build-isolation missing from release.yml build step'; exit 1)` — this is the same structural pattern already used by the module-size gate and CLI-reference drift gate in that workflow.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve before implementation whether `modelcontextprotocol/registry` GitHub Releases ship a per-artifact checksum file, and update the plan to state the definitive path (likely: hard-code SHA-256 computed at pin time) rather than leaving it as an open conditional.
findings.finding: The plan makes an unverified capability assertion about whether `modelcontextprotocol/registry` GitHub Releases ship a per-artifact `checksums.txt` or `.sha256` file. The plan says 'Prefer an upstream `checksums.txt` if `modelcontextprotocol/registry` ships one; otherwise hard-code the SHA-256' — but does not resolve which path actually applies. This is a load-bearing fact: if upstream ships no checksums file (the common case for newer projects), the 'otherwise' branch is the only real path, and the plan's preference ordering is inverted relative to reality.
findings.location: What section — 'MCP publisher — download → verify → execute' subsection; AC line for MCP publisher pin

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify before finalising the plan whether `modelcontextprotocol/registry` GitHub Releases ship a `checksums.txt` (or equivalent per-artifact digest file), and resolve the branch: if yes, specify the fetch+parse flow; if no, specify the hard-coded per-arch digest table approach — removing the deferred 'Prefer … if … ships one; otherwise' ambiguity.
findings.finding: The plan defers — without resolving — whether `modelcontextprotocol/registry` GitHub Releases ship a per-artifact checksum file (e.g. `checksums.txt` or `.sha256`). The plan says 'Prefer an upstream checksums.txt if `modelcontextprotocol/registry` ships one; otherwise hard-code the SHA-256 computed at pin time.' This is an unverified external capability that determines which implementation path the executor takes. If upstream does ship a `checksums.txt`, the verification step should fetch and parse it (a different curl + parse flow); if not, the SHA must be hard-coded per arch (compounding the arch-specificity finding above). Neither path is fully specified in the plan.
findings.location: What section — MCP publisher verification; AC line: 'If upstream ships no checksums, the pinned SHA-256 is hard-coded in the workflow with a rationale comment'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Remove the 'uv / pip pinning pattern' dual-track framing from the What section and state the prescription directly as pip-tools (`pip-compile --generate-hashes`), with uv as an acknowledged alternative only if the team has an existing preference — confirmed against the zero-uv-in-CI codebase baseline.
findings.finding: The 'What' section labels the approach 'Adopt the uv / pip pinning pattern (uv: full-SHA action pins, persist-credentials: false, a pinned release tool with a verified SHA-256; pip: SHA-pinned actions + a hash-locked build environment…)' — introducing 'uv' as a co-equal track. But uv has no CI footprint in this repo (only appears in docs/local-dev-env.md as a local-dev optional), and the plan immediately collapses to pip-tools (`pip-compile --generate-hashes`) as the sole prescription. This cargo-cult naming borrows trendy uv branding without any actual uv requirement, risking an implementer spending time on uv infrastructure that the plan does not actually call for.
findings.location: What section, first paragraph: 'Adopt the uv / pip pinning pattern (uv: full-SHA action pins…; pip: SHA-pinned actions + a hash-locked build environment installed with --require-hashes --no-deps)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'Adopt the uv / pip pinning pattern (uv: full-SHA action pins, persist-credentials: false, a pinned release tool with a verified SHA-256; pip: SHA-pinned actions + a hash-locked build environment installed with --require-hashes --no-deps)' with 'Adopt the pip-tools pinning pattern: full-SHA action pins, persist-credentials: false, a pinned release tool with a verified SHA-256, and a hash-locked build environment installed with --require-hashes --no-deps (generated by pip-compile --generate-hashes).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Rename the 'uv / pip pinning pattern' label (or rewrite the opening sentence) to reflect that `release.yml` is a pip-only workflow — uv is only an alternative lock-generation tool, not part of the workflow being changed.
findings.finding: The plan opens the 'What' section with 'Adopt the uv / pip pinning pattern (uv: full-SHA action pins, `persist-credentials: false`, a pinned release tool with a verified SHA-256; pip: SHA-pinned actions + a hash-locked build environment …)'. The release pipeline (`release.yml`) uses pip exclusively — there is no uv in the release workflow. Calling this a 'uv / pip pinning pattern' implies uv is part of the implemented approach, when uv is only mentioned parenthetically as an alternative lock generator (`pip-compile --generate-hashes` OR `uv pip compile --generate-hashes`). This label introduces prior-art confusion: an implementer may ask 'which uv actions do I SHA-pin?' when the answer is 'none — the label is incorrect for this codebase'.
findings.location: What section — opening paragraph
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the opening with: 'Adopt the standard supply-chain pinning pattern for pip-based release workflows: SHA-pin all GitHub Actions, set `persist-credentials: false`, and install build tools from a hash-locked requirements file generated by `pip-compile --generate-hashes` (or equivalently `uv pip compile --generate-hashes`).' Remove the 'uv: … pip: …' split which implies two separate implementation tracks.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State a specific decided path for the release lock file (e.g. `.github/release-requirements.txt`) rather than leaving it as 'e.g. under .github/ or requirements/'.
findings.finding: The lock/constraints file location is given as 'e.g. under `.github/` or `requirements/`' without a stated default — an executor must make an undirected choice, which creates merge friction with the concurrent Finding 1 and 3 stories editing the same workflow.
findings.location: Scope section — 'A new release build lock/constraints file (e.g. under `.github/` or `requirements/`)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'e.g. under `.github/` or `requirements/`' with a single decided path such as `.github/release-requirements.txt`, matching the proximity convention already used by other workflow support files.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion requiring a CI job that actually runs `pip install --require-hashes -r <lock> && python -m build --no-build-isolation` against a test package or the real source, proving the end-to-end locked build path succeeds before the change is merged.
findings.finding: No acceptance criterion exercises the `--no-build-isolation` build path end-to-end in CI (actually running a wheel build with the pre-installed hash-locked env). AC4's lint step only checks the flag's syntactic presence in the workflow file; it cannot detect runtime failures where the pre-installed env is incomplete or the flag is silently ineffective.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4 and What section — '--no-build-isolation' strategy

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the plan's premise: acknowledge that SHA-pinning is partially (not uniformly) applied in the repo, and explicitly state whether `_optionality.yml`, `test.yml`, and other non-release workflows are out of scope for this story.
findings.finding: The plan's stated rationale — 'The repo already SHA-pins actions in `gerrit-verify.yaml` and `_optionality.yml`, so `release.yml` is inconsistent with the repo's own convention' — is materially inaccurate. `_optionality.yml` uses tag-form `actions/checkout@v7` (lines 47, 74, 145, 188) and `actions/setup-python@v6` (lines 55, 82, 153, 196), not SHA pins. `gerrit-verify.yaml` is mixed: some steps use SHA pins (e.g. `actions/setup-python@ece7cb06...` at line 141) but `actions/setup-python@v6` at line 75 is still a tag. The plan's own scope is correctly limited to `release.yml`, but the premise that SHA-pinning is the repo's 'existing convention' is overstated — it is partial and inconsistent.
findings.location: Why section: 'The repo already SHA-pins actions in `gerrit-verify.yaml` and `_optionality.yml`'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Why section, change 'The repo already SHA-pins actions in `gerrit-verify.yaml` and `_optionality.yml`' to 'Some jobs in `gerrit-verify.yaml` already SHA-pin third-party actions (e.g. `lfreleng-actions/*`); first-party actions (`actions/checkout`, `actions/setup-python`) remain tag-pinned across most workflows including `_optionality.yml` and `test.yml`. Pinning first-party actions in `release.yml` is the first step; companion stories should address the remaining workflows.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `.github/dependabot.yml` to Scope (or explicitly call it out-of-scope with rationale), and clarify in `docs/releasing.md`'s Pinning section how both action SHA bumps (Dependabot-notified) and Python build-tool lock updates (manual cadence) are handled.
findings.finding: `.github/dependabot.yml` is an unenumerated consumer of the action SHA-pinning change. It is configured for `package-ecosystem: github-actions` across `directory: /`, which includes `release.yml`. Once actions in `release.yml` are changed from mutable tags to full SHAs, Dependabot will generate advisory bump PRs for those SHA pins monthly. The plan's 'Update pins through reviewed dependency-update changes' section implicitly requires a policy for handling these Dependabot PRs, but `.github/dependabot.yml` is not named in Scope and no coordination is described.
findings.location: Scope section; What section: 'Update pins through reviewed dependency-update changes, not implicit resolution during a production release'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit note in Scope that the `docs/releasing.md` Supply-chain attestations section's literal `pypa/gh-action-pypi-publish@release/v1` reference should be updated to the SHA form (or to a human-readable version comment) alongside the Pinning section edits.
findings.finding: The `docs/releasing.md` 'Supply-chain attestations' section (line 45) contains the literal string `pypa/gh-action-pypi-publish@release/v1` — the mutable branch reference being replaced by this plan. After the SHA-pin change, this prose reference will be stale (it will not match the new SHA-pinned form in release.yml). The plan's Scope says it owns only the 'Pinning' section of `docs/releasing.md` and explicitly does not list the Supply-chain attestations section as needing an update.
findings.location: Scope section: 'docs/releasing.md — document the pinning policy and the pin-update process. Section ownership: this story owns the "Pinning" section only'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Supply-chain attestations section update, change the prose reference from `pypa/gh-action-pypi-publish@release/v1` to `pypa/gh-action-pypi-publish@<SHA>  # release/v1` to stay consistent with the new SHA-pin convention.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC to the leaf (or a Scope note) that explicitly acknowledges the parent epic's requirement: plan-review advisory findings must be recorded with Accept/Reject disposition and rationale as a ticket comment, and any Accepted findings reflected in the description, before the Gerrit LLM-Review +1 / Verified +1 votes.
findings.finding: The parent epic's AC requires each child story to record plan-review advisory findings with an explicit Accept/Reject disposition and one-line rationale as a ticket comment, with every Accepted finding reflected in the child's description before the Gerrit LLM-Review+1/Verified+1 votes are cast. The leaf's ACs, What, and Scope sections contain no mention of this process obligation, leaving the parent-mandated gate condition unrepresented in the leaf's definition of done.
findings.location: Leaf Acceptance Criteria (all seven ACs) / Parent AC: 'Each child story's plan-review advisory findings are recorded with an explicit Accept/Reject disposition and a one-line rationale (as a comment on the child ticket)…'
findings.suggested_fix: Append to the leaf's Acceptance Criteria: '- [ ] Plan-review advisory findings for this story are recorded on this ticket with an explicit Accept/Reject disposition and one-line rationale (as a ticket comment); every Accepted finding is reflected in this description before the Gerrit LLM-Review +1 / Verified +1 votes are cast. (Required by parent epic 2610-9557-c3c1-460c AC.)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reference the existing acli download-verify-execute pattern in `external-integration.yml:76-100` as the prior art the MCP publisher step should model, and call out any intentional divergences.
findings.finding: The plan's MCP publisher verification pattern (3-step download→verify→execute) is functionally identical to the pattern the repo already uses for acli in `external-integration.yml:76-100` and `reconcile-bridge.yml:164-166`, but the plan does not reference this prior art. This matters because the existing acli pattern has solved the same problems (exact-version URL, sha256sum -c --strict, separate download/verify/extract steps, error messaging) — failing to reference it means the implementer may diverge from the established repo convention rather than extend it.
findings.location: What section — 'MCP publisher — download → verify → execute' subsection
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence in the 'MCP publisher' subsection: 'Model this on the existing `Download + verify + install acli` step in `external-integration.yml` — same curl-to-file, sha256sum -c --strict, tar-separate sequence already reviewed in this repo.'

findings.finding: The Scope section's parenthetical rationale for leaving `pyproject.toml` unchanged — '(the lock governs the isolated build env)' — directly contradicts the What section's technical explanation, which states that pip's default `python -m build` resolves `[build-system]` requires in a fresh isolated env so 'the lock only governs Hatchling's version if the build uses the pre-installed locked env'. An implementer reading only the Scope section will believe the lock already covers the isolated build env with no further action, while the What section correctly explains that a pyproject.toml pin or `--no-build-isolation` is still required.
findings.location: Scope section — `pyproject.toml` bullet; What section — build isolation paragraph
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the Scope `pyproject.toml` bullet's parenthetical with: 'no standalone change is required *if* `--no-build-isolation` is chosen; a version pin in `[build-system]` requires is needed if the isolated-build path is kept — see What section for the enforcing mechanism choice.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define an explicit coordination contract for the three concurrent stories that share `release.yml` — e.g. a designated merge order (Finding 1 → Finding 3 → this story), a shared branch, or a workflow-section ownership map that partitions edits — so no story silently overwrites another's changes.
findings.finding: The plan acknowledges that Findings 1, 3, and this story all modify `release.yml` concurrently, but the only coordination mechanism stated is 'coordinate with Findings 1 and 3' — no merge order, sequencing contract, or interface boundary (e.g. a shared workflow-editing checklist, feature-branch merge order, or split-file convention) is described to prevent conflicting edits from producing an inconsistent workflow state.
findings.location: Scope section: 'coordinate with Findings 1 and 3, which also edit this file'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Coordination' sub-section to Scope that specifies either (a) a merge order (e.g. this story is a prerequisite of Finding 1 and 3 and must land first), or (b) that all three stories are implemented in a single branch with a single review, or (c) that `release.yml` is partitioned into composable reusable workflow files so each story owns a distinct file.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add explicit timeouts (`curl --max-time`/`--connect-timeout`, or a `timeout` wrapper for pip install) to all external-network steps, and specify that a timeout exits non-zero immediately with a diagnostic message (e.g., echo the actual downloaded digest before failing the sha256sum step) so operators can distinguish a hang from a hash mismatch.
findings.finding: The plan specifies external network operations (curl download of the MCP publisher, pip install from PyPI) with no timeout or hang-detection, meaning a slow or unresponsive endpoint silently consumes runner time and can block a release indefinitely with no circuit-breaker or degraded-mode fallback.
findings.location: What section — MCP publisher steps 1–3; Release build tools pip install step
findings.suggested_fix: For curl: use `curl -fsSL --max-time 120 --connect-timeout 30 ...`. For pip: wrap with `timeout 300 pip install --require-hashes ...`. On sha256sum failure, emit the actual digest with `sha256sum mcp-publisher.tar.gz` before exiting, so the failure is diagnosable in the log.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a specified procedure for independently verifying the MCP publisher SHA-256 before committing it — e.g., require a second independent download (different network/machine), comparison against an upstream signed release manifest or GitHub-attested artifact, or a reproducible build cross-check — and document this in `docs/releasing.md` under the Pinning section.
findings.finding: The plan mandates hard-coding the MCP publisher SHA-256 'computed at pin time' but does not specify a procedure for independently verifying that value before committing it. If the developer who computes the SHA downloads the binary from the same (potentially compromised or MITMed) channel used at run time, the hard-coded digest provides no additional trust guarantee — it only detects accidental corruption, not a targeted substitution at pin time.
findings.location: What section — MCP publisher bullet: 'otherwise hard-code the SHA-256 computed at pin time with a rationale comment'; AC line 4 — MCP publisher digest verification
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'MCP publisher' bullet and in `docs/releasing.md`, add: 'When no upstream checksums.txt exists, two independent developers (or one developer from two separate networks/machines) must each download the exact-version archive and confirm matching SHA-256 values before the hash is committed; the rationale comment in the workflow must record the verification method used (e.g., "cross-checked against GitHub release asset on YYYY-MM-DD by @author and @reviewer").' Alternatively, prefer the upstream checksums.txt path and specify that it must be cryptographically signed (e.g., via Sigstore/cosign attestation) when available.

findings.finding: AC3 requires 'two builds at the same source SHA resolving byte-identical tool versions (recorded in release evidence)' but no step in the plan describes how this cross-build comparison is performed, what command produces it, or when in the release process it is executed — making it unverifiable in-session.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC3
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'two builds at the same source SHA resolving byte-identical tool versions' with an in-session check: 'The release workflow step that installs build tools prints `pip list --format=freeze > tool-versions.txt` and attaches it as a release artifact; a lint step in CI asserts the installed versions match the lock file exactly (diff against pinned versions extracted from the lock).'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
